text:
In this paper we deal with the risks and obstacles encountered by journalists during the Israel-Gaza war that broke out in October 2023. Based on the Hierarchy of Influences Model, this study investigates the various factors that affect the reporting of conflict from the war zone over time, spanning individual, interpersonal, organisational, social, and societal parameters. The war between Israel and Gaza in October 2023 was the ﬁrst war in which media coverage was extensively influenced by social media and new media channels, while at the same time the numerous acts of terror committed against Israeli civilians continued non-stop and Israel launched extensive military operations against terrorist organisations in the Gaza Strip. Journalism is a core element of democratic reporting and serves as a source of information, acting as a tool that strengthens the population's ability to hold decision-makers accountable. This study aims to illustrate the extraordinary challenges faced by journalists reporting from the war zone during this period, and to examine which variables from the model constructed by Weaver & Gastmann Attinger (2002) serve as triggers and inﬂuence the extent of exposure to harm, as well as the representation of reality as it appears in war reports.

The research report was based on in-depth interviews with 18 Palestinian journalists operating in the Gaza Strip. The aim of the research was to document the working experiences of these journalists by describing and analyzing the pressures they encounter. These pressures are structural, professional, social, political, civic, workplace, as well as personal. They are related to the risks the journalists have to endure and the requirements they must fulfill, such as their working hours, the stories they have to cover, their working conditions, as well as the internal and external pressures to which they are exposed within their newsroom and outside it.

Iran Daily reports that the study showed that the traditional hierarchy of factors that affect media freedom in Iran was applicable to a certain extent in a semi-integrated way, with personal factors on one side and structural on the other, overlapping in many cases. The study also discussed the reporter’s professional need to remain neutral versus their personal experience as a victim. It further described the impact of new media technologies as a “bitter pill” with advantages and disadvantages. According to the study, the new media provided a forum that could not be imagined without the freedom of cyberspace. At the same time, the free online space has led to a variety of forms of self-censorship, as well as harassment of the journalists and the websites and increased vulnerability to arrests and restrictions on their activities.

Practically this work sheds light on the importance of the social and technical measures that protect journalists and media professionals in dangerous areas of the world. Socially it also significantly enriches the discussion of media ethics, particularly with regard to the human cost of war reporting. Connecting theory and research with the lived experience of war reporting, specifically in the context of the Israel–Gaza conflict, this study offers a first comprehensive and innovative perspective on the mechanisms of contemporary war reporting, and how journalists cope with the immense challenges of covering extreme forms of violence.
